section.title: The Resurrection of the Dead
section.sectionRationale: Having laid down the resurrection of Christ as bedrock, Paul now confronts those in Corinth who deny a resurrection of the dead. He shows that the two stand or fall together: if the dead are not raised, then Christ is not raised, and the whole faith collapses into futility. But Christ has been raised, the firstfruits, and His resurrection guarantees the harvest. As in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive, until He has put every enemy, last of all death, under His feet, and God is all in all.
section.thesis: The resurrection of Christ and the resurrection of His people cannot be separated: deny one and you lose both, and with them all hope; but Christ has truly been raised as the firstfruits, so that as in Adam all die, in Christ all will be made alive, and the risen Lord will reign until the last enemy, death, is destroyed and God is all in all.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the collapse if Christ is not raised; but Christ is raised, the firstfruits, undoing Adam's death; the reign of Christ until every enemy, last of all death, is subdued; and the call to holy living in light of the resurrection hope.

units.label: If Christ has not been raised

verses.text: But if it is preached that Christ has been {{egeiro|raised}} from the dead, how can some of you say that there is no {{anastasis|resurrection}} of the dead?
If there is no resurrection of the dead, then not even Christ has been raised.
And if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is worthless, and so is your faith.
In that case, we are also exposed as false witnesses about God. For we have testified about God that He raised Christ from the dead, but He did not raise Him if in fact the dead are not raised.
For if the dead are not raised, then not even Christ has been raised.
And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile; you are still in your sins.
Then those also who have fallen asleep in Christ have perished.
If our hope in Christ is for this life alone, we are to be pitied more than all men.

units.label: But Christ has been raised, the firstfruits
units.note: in Adam all die, in Christ all made alive

verses.text: But Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the {{aparche|firstfruits}} of those who have fallen asleep.
For since {{thanatos|death}} came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man.
For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.
But each in his own turn: Christ the firstfruits; then at His coming, those who belong to Him.

units.label: He must reign until death is destroyed
units.note: every enemy under His feet, God all in all

verses.text: Then the end will come, when He hands over the kingdom to God the Father after He has destroyed all dominion, authority, and power.
For He must reign until He has put all His enemies under His feet.
The last enemy to be destroyed is {{thanatos|death}}.
For “God has put everything under His feet.” Now when it says that everything has been put under Him, this clearly does not include the One who put everything under Him.
And when all things have been subjected to Him, then the Son Himself will be made subject to Him who put all things under Him, so that God may be all in all.

units.label: Live in light of the resurrection
units.note: why risk everything, and stop sinning

verses.text: If these things are not so, what will those do who are baptized for the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why are people baptized for them?
And why do we endanger ourselves every hour?
I face death every day, brothers, as surely as I boast about you in Christ Jesus our Lord.
If I fought wild beasts in Ephesus for human motives, what did I gain? If the dead are not raised, “Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.”
Do not be deceived: “Bad company corrupts good character.”
Sober up as you ought, and stop sinning; for some of you are ignorant of God. I say this to your shame.

egeiro.gloss: To raise, to wake, to lift up. The drumbeat verb of the chapter. Paul rings the change on it again and again: Christ has been raised, and if the dead are not raised then not even Christ has been raised. The resurrection of Christ and of His people rise and fall together on this one word.

anastasis.gloss: A standing up, a rising, resurrection. The bodily raising of the dead to new life, not merely the survival of the soul. Some at Corinth denied there is any such thing; Paul insists that to deny the resurrection of the dead is to deny the resurrection of Christ, and so to empty the gospel.

aparche.gloss: Firstfruits, the first portion of the harvest offered to God as the pledge and guarantee of the full crop to come. To call the risen Christ the firstfruits is to say His resurrection is the first installment and sure promise of the resurrection of all who belong to Him; where the firstfruits have been raised, the harvest must follow.

thanatos.gloss: Death. In Paul death is not merely natural but an enemy, the last foe of God's people, the reign of the grave that entered through Adam's sin. The whole argument marches toward one triumphant claim: the last enemy to be destroyed is death, undone by the risen and reigning Christ.

basic.context: Some in Corinth were saying there is no resurrection of the dead. Paul shows they cannot hold that and still keep the gospel. If the dead are never raised, then Christ was never raised either, and if Christ was not raised, then the preaching is empty, faith is worthless, and believers are still lost in their sins. But Christ has been raised, and that changes everything. He is the firstfruits, the first of a great harvest. As death spread from Adam to all, so life spreads from Christ to all who are His.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul argues by drawing out consequences. He takes the Corinthian denial to its logical end to show how ruinous it is, then turns the corner with a triumphant but Christ has indeed been raised. He reasons from Adam to Christ, from firstfruits to harvest, and from the reign of Christ to the final destruction of death, ending with the practical point that if there is no resurrection, holiness and sacrifice make no sense.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter hammers the verb egeiro, to raise, again and again. The disputed word is anastasis, resurrection, the bodily rising some denied. Christ is the aparche, the firstfruits, the first sheaf that guarantees the whole harvest. And the great enemy is thanatos, death, which entered through Adam and will be the last foe destroyed by the reigning Christ.
basic.typology: Christ the firstfruits fulfills the Old Testament feast of firstfruits, when the first sheaf of harvest was offered as the pledge of the full crop. Adam and Christ stand as two heads of humanity: through the first, death; through the last, life. The reign of Christ until every enemy is under His feet fulfills the enthronement promised in Psalm 8 and Psalm 110.
basic.christ: Christ is the raised one on whom everything hangs. His resurrection is not an isolated wonder but the firstfruits, the beginning of the harvest of the dead. He is the new head of the human race, the last Adam, who undoes what the first Adam did. And He is the reigning Lord who must rule until every enemy, including death itself, is placed under His feet, before He hands the kingdom to the Father.
basic.god: It is God who raised Christ and who will raise us. God is putting everything under Christ's feet, and in the end the Son will hand the kingdom to the Father, so that God may be all in all. The goal of the whole drama is the unclouded reign of God over a redeemed creation, with death, the last enemy, forever destroyed.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 5 sets Adam and Christ side by side, death through one and life through the other. Psalm 110 and Psalm 8 promise a Lord who reigns until His enemies are His footstool. Revelation 20 and 21 show death itself thrown into the lake of fire and God dwelling with His people. Second Corinthians 4 speaks of the risen life that makes present suffering worth it.
basic.oneStory: This passage carries the Bible's story to its end. The death that entered through Adam meets its match in the risen Christ, the firstfruits of a new humanity. His resurrection guarantees ours, and His reign will not end until the last enemy, death, is destroyed and God is all in all. What began in a garden with a curse ends with a King who has emptied the grave.

context.literary: Verses 12 to 19 run a chain of if-then arguments that dismantle the Corinthian denial, piling up the ruin that follows from it. Verse 20 pivots with a triumphant but, and verses 20 to 28 build the positive case: firstfruits, Adam and Christ, the ordered stages, and the reign of Christ until death is destroyed. Verses 29 to 34 apply the doctrine to life, arguing that risk, sacrifice, and holiness only make sense if the dead are raised.
context.historical: The Corinthian denial likely grew from Greek ideas that prized the immortal soul and despised the body, so that a future bodily resurrection seemed crude or unnecessary. Paul does not accept a bodiless afterlife as the hope of the gospel. The reference to baptism for the dead and to fighting wild beasts in Ephesus reflects local practices and Paul's own hardships that his readers would recognize.
context.canonical: This is the fullest treatment of resurrection in the New Testament. Its Adam and Christ contrast expands what Romans 5 states, its firstfruits image draws on the Levitical feasts, and its vision of Christ reigning until every enemy is subdued weaves together Psalm 8 and Psalm 110 into the New Testament's picture of the end, echoed in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To refute the denial of the resurrection of the dead by showing it destroys the resurrection of Christ and the entire gospel, to establish the risen Christ as the firstfruits and last Adam whose resurrection guarantees ours, and to call the church to holy, hopeful living in light of it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The bulk of the passage is argument and doctrine, establishing what is true about resurrection. The prescriptive turn comes at the end: sober up, stop sinning, do not be led astray. The reality of the resurrection grounds a real call to holiness and courage now.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by those who are baptized for the dead in verse 29?

views.label: An argument from a practice Paul does not endorse
views.case: Paul refers to something some were doing, whatever it was, to expose its inconsistency: it makes no sense to be baptized for the dead if the dead are never raised. He argues from their own practice without commending it, and notably switches to they and them rather than we.

views.label: One of several proposed meanings
views.case: Others read it as baptism undertaken in hope of reunion with dead believers, or as normal baptism understood in relation to the dead. The wording is genuinely obscure, and the church has never agreed on the precise reference.

debates.bearing: On any reading, the verse does not establish a doctrine of vicarious baptism that saves the dead. Paul is not teaching salvation by proxy but pressing home that resurrection hope alone makes such actions coherent; the rest of Scripture ties salvation to personal faith in Christ, not to being baptized on someone else's behalf.

debates.question: How can the Son be subjected to the Father in verse 28 without being lesser than the Father?

views.label: The Son's saving mission, not His nature
views.case: The subjection describes the Son's role in accomplishing redemption as the incarnate mediator, handing the completed kingdom to the Father; it speaks of order and mission within the Godhead, not of any inequality of being.

views.label: A picture of the goal of redemption
views.case: The verse paints the final state in which God is all in all, the whole redeemed order gladly under God's unclouded reign through the Son, rather than making a metaphysical claim that the Son is inferior.

debates.bearing: The orthodox faith confesses the Son as fully and eternally God, equal with the Father; verse 28 describes the consummation of His saving work, not a lowering of His deity.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is built on four words. Egeiro, to raise, tolls through the argument like a bell: Christ has been raised, and if the dead are not raised then not even Christ has been raised. The contested term is anastasis, resurrection, the bodily rising that some denied while still claiming the gospel. Against them Paul sets aparche, firstfruits, the first sheaf offered to God as the pledge of the whole harvest, so that the risen Christ is the guarantee of His people's rising. And the great foe is thanatos, death, which came through Adam and reigns over the grave, yet will be the last enemy destroyed by the reigning Christ, until God is all in all.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the feast of firstfruits in Leviticus 23, when the first sheaf of harvest was offered to God as the pledge of the full crop; the risen Christ is the first sheaf guaranteeing the harvest of the dead.
Christ answers and reverses the first Adam: through Adam came sin and death for all, through Christ comes righteousness and life for all who are His.

typology.type: The reigning King with enemies under His feet
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills Psalm 8 and Psalm 110, the man crowned with glory and the Lord who reigns until His enemies are made His footstool, realized in the risen Christ.

study.christ: Christ is the center and the guarantee. His resurrection is not a solitary miracle but the firstfruits of a great harvest, the first installment that secures the whole. He is the last Adam, the new head of humanity, who undoes the ruin of the first: where Adam brought death to all, Christ brings life to all who are His. And He is the reigning Lord, who must rule until every hostile power and the last enemy, death, are placed under His feet, whereupon He hands the kingdom to the Father. To deny the resurrection is to lose this Christ altogether; to confess it is to have Him as the pledge of our own rising.
study.god: God stands over the whole scene as the one who raised Christ and will raise us, the one who is subjecting all things to the Son, and the one to whom the Son will hand the completed kingdom. The end of the story is that God may be all in all, His reign unclouded, His enemies destroyed, death itself abolished. Here is a God who does not leave His people to the grave but overthrows death and fills all things with His life.

crossReferences.note: Adam and Christ as two heads: death reigns through the one, grace and life abound through the other, expanding verses 21 to 22.
The Lord seated at God's right hand until His enemies are made His footstool, behind verse 25's He must reign.
God has put everything under His feet, quoted in verse 27 and applied to the reign of the risen Christ.
Death and Hades are thrown into the lake of fire, the destruction of the last enemy that verse 26 promises.
Those who have fallen asleep in Christ will rise at His coming, matching each in his own turn in verse 23.

study.oneStory: Here the Bible's long war with death reaches its turning point. Death entered through one man, Adam, and spread to all; life enters through one man, Christ, and reaches all who belong to Him. The risen Jesus is the firstfruits, the sure pledge that the harvest of the dead will follow, and He reigns now until the last enemy, death, is destroyed and the kingdom is handed to the Father. The story that began with a curse in a garden ends with God all in all, and the grave forever emptied.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say the resurrection of the dead cannot be denied?

options.text: Because if the dead are not raised, then not even Christ has been raised, and the gospel collapses.
options.note: The two rise and fall together; deny one and you lose the other.

options.text: Because the resurrection is promised throughout the Old Testament.
options.note: There is Old Testament hope of resurrection, but Paul's point here is that denying it denies Christ's own resurrection.

options.text: Because Greek philosophy required it.
options.note: Greek thought tended to deny bodily resurrection; Paul argues against that.

options.text: Because the dead have already been seen rising.
options.note: Paul grounds it in Christ's resurrection, not in observed risings of others.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Christ is the firstfruits?

options.text: His resurrection is the first installment and sure pledge of the resurrection of all who belong to Him.
options.note: Where the firstfruits are raised, the harvest must follow.

options.text: He was simply the first person ever to come back to life.
options.note: Others were revived before, but firstfruits means His rising guarantees ours.

options.text: He is the best example of a good life.
options.note: Firstfruits is about resurrection and harvest, not merely example.

options.text: His resurrection has nothing to do with ours.
options.note: The whole point is that His rising secures ours.

quiz.q: How does Paul contrast Adam and Christ?

options.text: As in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.
options.note: Two heads of humanity: death through one, life through the other.

options.text: Adam brought sin and Christ brought forgiveness, but not new life.
options.note: Partly right, yet Paul's stress falls on death through Adam and resurrection life through Christ.

options.text: Adam brought life and Christ brought death.
options.note: It is the reverse: death through Adam, life through Christ.

options.text: Both simply teach us to live well.
options.note: The contrast is about death and resurrection life, not mere teaching.

quiz.q: What is the last enemy to be destroyed?

options.text: Death.
options.note: The last enemy to be destroyed is death, verse 26.

options.text: Human governments.
options.note: Christ subdues all rule and authority, but the last enemy named is death.

options.text: Satan alone.
options.note: Hostile powers are subdued, but Paul names death as the final enemy.

options.text: Nothing; the world simply continues as it is.
options.note: Paul promises the destruction of death, not the status quo.

quiz.q: How should the resurrection shape the way believers live?

options.text: It grounds courage, sacrifice, and holy living, so we sober up and stop sinning.
options.note: If the dead are raised, risk and holiness make sense; verses 32 to 34.

options.text: It means we can live for pleasure since nothing matters.
options.note: That is the very attitude Paul rejects, let us eat and drink.

options.text: It means we should withdraw from all danger.
options.note: Paul embraces daily danger precisely because of the resurrection.

options.text: It mainly comforts us about the future but changes little now.
options.note: It does give future comfort, yet Paul draws present consequences: courage, sacrifice, and holiness.

quiz.q: How should we understand baptism for the dead in verse 29?

options.text: Paul argues from a practice without endorsing it, and the verse does not teach salvation by proxy.
options.note: He presses its inconsistency if the dead are not raised; Scripture ties salvation to personal faith.

options.text: It establishes that we can be saved through someone else's baptism.
options.note: The passage does not teach vicarious baptism for salvation.

options.text: The meaning is genuinely obscure and disputed.
options.note: True, the reference is unclear, but Paul's point is that resurrection alone makes such acts coherent.

options.text: It shows the early church practiced baptism for the dead as a model for us.
options.note: Paul mentions a practice but does not commend it as a model to follow.

questions: Paul says that without the resurrection, faith is futile and we are still in our sins. How does the certainty of Christ's resurrection change the weight of your daily struggles?
Christ is the firstfruits, the guarantee of the harvest. How does knowing your own resurrection is secured in His shape the way you face loss and death?
The risen Christ reigns until every enemy is under His feet. Where do you need to trust that the powers troubling you are already being subdued?
If there were no resurrection, Paul says we may as well eat and drink for tomorrow we die. How does resurrection hope free you to sacrifice and take risks for Christ?
Paul ends by calling the church to sober up and stop sinning. What would it look like for resurrection hope to make you more, not less, serious about holiness?

translationNotes.note: Firstfruits translates aparche, the first portion of harvest offered to God as a pledge of the full crop; the image guarantees the resurrection of all who belong to Christ.

Baptized for the dead is one of the most debated phrases in the letter. Paul reasons from a practice without endorsing it, and switches to they and them; the verse does not establish a doctrine of saving baptism performed on behalf of the dead.
Fought wild beasts in Ephesus is likely a vivid figure for fierce human opposition rather than a literal arena; the quoted line, let us eat and drink, echoes Isaiah 22:13.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 15:12-34. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
